questions.question: Loudness is the human perception of a sound's:
questions.explanation: Loudness is how loud or soft a sound seems — it is determined by the amplitude of the sound wave.

questions.question: A sound wave with a larger amplitude is perceived as:
questions.explanation: Greater amplitude means more energy in the wave, which the ear interprets as a louder sound.

questions.question: On a sound waveform display, a louder sound is represented by:
options: A taller wave (greater displacement from the midline)
A shorter wave (smaller displacement from the midline)
More waves (more cycles) packed into the same time interval
Fewer waves (fewer cycles) spread across the display
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the height of the wave from the midline. A taller wave means greater amplitude and a louder sound.

questions.question: As you move farther from a sound source, the sound becomes quieter because:
options: The frequency of the sound waves drops lower over long distances.
The sound energy spreads out across a much larger surface area.
The physical speed of the sound waves decreases as they travel out.
The wavelength of the sound waves stretches and increases in size.
questions.explanation: Sound energy spreads out as it travels. The same total energy distributed over a larger area means less intensity at any one point.

questions.question: A sound wave with greater amplitude carries:
options: The same energy as a quieter sound
questions.explanation: Amplitude is related to the energy carried by the wave. Larger amplitude means the wave transfers more energy.

questions.question: The decibel (dB) scale measures:
questions.explanation: The decibel scale is a logarithmic scale used to measure sound intensity level — how loud a sound is perceived.

questions.question: The quietest sound that the average human ear can detect is defined as:
questions.explanation: $0\text{ dB}$ is the threshold of hearing — the faintest sound detectable by a healthy human ear.

questions.question: Prolonged exposure to sound levels above approximately $85\text{ dB}$ can cause:
options: A change in the speed of sound
questions.explanation: Sustained exposure to sounds above $85\text{ dB}$ can damage the delicate hair cells in the inner ear, leading to permanent hearing loss.

questions.question: A sound can be loud and low-pitched. This shows that:
options: Loudness and pitch are the associated properties
Loudness and pitch are independent properties
Loudness and pitch have an inverse or indirect relationship
Loudness and pitch are properties of sound
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude; pitch depends on frequency. A sound can have any combination of loud/soft and high/low pitch, proving they are independent. While D is a true statement, it does not answer what the example specifically demonstrates.

questions.question: Two sounds have the same pitch but different loudness. On an oscilloscope, they would show:
options: The same amplitude but different frequencies
The same frequency but different amplitudes
The same amplitude and the same frequency
questions.explanation: Same pitch means same frequency. Different loudness means different amplitude. The traces would have the same spacing between waves but different heights.

questions.question: A whisper is about $30\text{ dB}$ and a shout is about $80\text{ dB}$. The shout has:
questions.explanation: A higher decibel level means greater intensity, which corresponds to greater amplitude. Speed and frequency are not necessarily different.

questions.question: The SI unit for measuring sound intensity level is the:
questions.explanation: Sound intensity level is measured in decibels (dB). Hertz measures frequency, meters measure distance, and m/s measures speed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered loudness, amplitude, and the decibel scale!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between amplitude (loudness) and frequency (pitch).
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on how distance affects loudness and the meaning of decibel levels.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: loudness = perception of amplitude, decibel scale, and factors affecting loudness.
